## Quantitative and Functional Ecology

Apa khabar! I'm Lai Hao Ran (賴浩然) and a plant community ecologist, currently jointly [appointed](cv.qmd#current-position) by South East Asia Rainforest Research Partnership ([SEARRP](https://www.searrp.org/)), Malaysia and Imperial College London, UK. My [research](#research-themes) aims to understand and generalise the dynamics of forest biodiversity through functional traits, statistical rigour, and long-term monitoring. My career goal is to build capacity and foster a more innovative research community in Malaysia and tropical Asia.

Please feel free to reach out for research collaboration, forest fieldwork, guest teaching, and project (co)supervision.

## Research themes

With selected publications.

### Functional trait ecology

Plant functional traits are a universal currency for understanding how species respond to their environment and shape the communities around them. My work uses traits to explain forest community assembly, successional dynamics, and biodiversity patterns across tropical and subtropical systems — from Borneo to New Zealand.

- Lai et al. (2020) [Functional traits that moderate tropical tree recruitment during post‐windstorm secondary succession](http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/1365-2745.13347){target='_blank'} *Journal of Ecology*

- Lai et al. (2021) [Successional syndromes of saplings in tropical secondary forests emerge from environment‐dependent trait–demography relationships](http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/ele.13784){target='_blank'} *Ecology Letters*

- Radford‐Smith et al. (2024) [A Functional Basis for the Assembly of Australian Subtropical Rainforest Tree Communities](http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/ele.70014){target='_blank'} *Ecology Letters*

### Species interactions and coexistence

Species do not exist in isolation: competitive, facilitative, and non-additive biotic interactions jointly determine who persists and where. I develop and apply frameworks from modern coexistence theory and ecological network science to quantify these interactions and understand how they propagate through communities under disturbance.

- Lai et al. (2021) [Non‐additive biotic interactions improve predictions of tropical tree growth and impact community size structure](http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/ecy.3588){target='_blank'} *Ecology*

- Martins et al. (2024) [The propagation of disturbances in ecological networks](http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.tree.2024.01.009){target='_blank'} *Trends in Ecology & Evolution*

- Lai et al. (2024) [Detecting Nonadditive Biotic Interactions and Assessing Their Biological Relevance among Temperate Rainforest Trees](http://dx.doi.org/10.1086/730807){target='_blank'} *The American Naturalist*

### Quantitative methods

Ecological inference is only as good as the models behind it. I develop and apply novel statistical approaches —- including hierarchical models, model-based ordination, and joint species distribution models —- to extract rigorous ecological signal from observational and long-term monitoring data.

- Lai (2025) [Model‐based ordination for phenological studies: From controlling sampling bias to inferring temporal associations](http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.70079){target='_blank'} *Methods in Ecology and Evolution*

- Dehling et al. (2025) [Eltonian Niche Modelling: Applying Joint Hierarchical Niche Models to Ecological Networks](http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/ele.70120){target='_blank'} *Ecology Letters*
